comments: TV says he is playing four tracks by Girlschool but only plays three, 'Take It All Away' not played.
Tony Wilson "A special goodnight to all teddy bears who fell asleep last week."

sessions.details: first session repeat TX, recorded May 1980.

track_listing.details: LP-The Dark Side of the Moon

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well hello there, this is TV on the Radio, Thomas the Vance here, and welcome once again to the program called The Friday Rock Show.
And this evening we repeat the session recorded last May by Girl School, and out of the archives, recorded in August 1977,
a set by Coliseum 2, the band that included at that time Gary Moore on guitar and Don Airy on keyboards.
We've got some classic cuts by Leonard Skinner, Robin Trau, Pink Floyd, Neil Young, among others.
New releases by Iron Maiden, Kansas, Straight 8, Status Quo, Alvin Lee and Wishbone Ash.
And a couple of live tracks recorded in Jackson, Florida by Blackfoot.
Not forgetting our Friday Night Connection to get you going.
But this evening we start with UFO.
[Music]
[Music]
And that was Leonard Skinner with a track from the album called [redacted address].
The track called Sweet Home Alabama.
And it was specially for Mick and Sue who were actually not together until August of this year.
I assume they are still together and I hope they're happy.
And also for a gentleman by the name of Kevin O'Shea,
lots of love from Maggie and a little love from Tony.
Peter Hilton wants me to say hello to all his friends
in Spennymoor and County Durham, they're Leonard Skinner fans.
Also would I say hello to Fiona McLeod and Penny Maltby
and Stripe the Blondie Freak, Sarah Gardner,
and me, me being Sarah Sanderson.
I'd say hello to Desmond Maxwell too,
who lives in Belfast, 14, 24, Spring...
Is that Springvale Road? Springvale Parade?
I know that part of the world.
And also for the Dinosaurs of Shildon.
That's Mike and Paul and Sam and Shanks and Steve.
And before that, you heard something by UFO.
You'll find that on the B-side of their new single.
The A-side is called Hot and...
Oh, I couldn't get it right, and it obviously is the fact tonight.
The B-side was Hot and Ready.
That's what you heard.
It was recorded live and ready.
And the UFO cut was for Mel the Shanker Freak.
That's Mel Parry and all the slave lads who live in Ben Lech in Anglesey.
And I'd like to say hello to everybody from U-4th High School.
Keep up the good music, says Eric Swinton of Kilkakadi and Fife.
And hello to Jackie.
I must tell Jackie that she is well-missed and well-loved by somebody called Snow Dog.
10, 14 and a half, and here's the first of four by Girl School.
Really hope that band never break up.
[Music]
[Music]
They put together such an amazing groove.
That was Robin Trauer from the album Caravan to Midnight.
The track was I'm Out to Get You.
And that was played most especially for Gary Chippet,
who lives very happily, I'm told, at [redacted address],
all on Melbourne in Peterborough.
I have to tell you that the family loves you,
and the family is Lorraine, Tisha and Joe.
Happy birthday, Gary.
Sounds like you've got yourself an ace family.
Also, I'd like to dedicate that track to a lady by the name of Jenny Piercy,
who attends the Lane Theatre Dancing College in Epsom in Surrey
with much love from Gary Robinson,
who lives at [redacted address], Thetford Road, Ingham, Bury St. Edmunds.
Before that, you heard the first of four tonight from Girls' School,
a session that we put together on the Friday Rock Show last May.
Kim was doing the lead vocals, the track was called Nothing To Lose,
and that was for all the staff of the RHQ Welsh Guards
from Gerry Sinners Sinstad, who left the Welsh Guards yesterday,
and also not forgetting his fiancée, whose name is Linda Wanstall.
It was nice to know you, Gerry. Good luck in Civis.
25 minutes after 10 on the Friday Rock Show.
In a few weeks' time, we're going to be doing our second birthday programme.
maybe you remember last year's, we called it Now We Are One.
This one we're going to call Two's Up.
And what we'd like to do is to invite you to write down just one track,
your all-time favourite rock track, on a separate postcard
to any other sort of involvement we ask you to become involved in.
A separate postcard, your all-time favourite rock track,
and send it to Two's Up, The Friday Rock Show,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A, 4WW.
And on our second birthday, we'll broadcast the top rock tracks of the nation.
This is Iron Maiden.
Coliseum 2, as they were in the studio on Monday the 15th of August 1977,
[Music]
when the lineup included Gary Moore on lead guitar, Don Airey on the keyboards,
John Mull on the bass, and John Eisenman on the drums.
Great band they used to be, great band.
The track was put it this way, and we played it specially for a gentleman
who's recently started his second year at the University of Exeter,
a gentleman called Andrew Bailey.
And somebody else who wanted us to dig that session out of the archives was Mark Treville, I think it is, or Teville, possibly,
who lives at [redacted address], St. Nicholas at Wade, Birchington in Kent.
And before that, you heard the new single by Iron Maiden, a single called Women in Uniform.
Kansas have a new album out. Here's a track from it.
It's from the dark side of the moon, Pink Floyd and money.
[Music]
[Music]
[Music]
And that was for Alvin the Australian, Jeff the Sad Cafe Head.
Next, the bass bin.
I like that.
Tony the Axeman, Mervyn the Berman, Mike Lepia,
Deb the Big, Bridget the Bigger,
and all the girls at Hengrove Fatty Class Number,
I think it is here.
Not forgetting somebody by the name of Stephen Griffiths
and Ian Bartlett from Whitchurch in Bristol.
And that particular track was also for Richard,
who's just gone away to Bristol University.
Fond regards from somebody who's obviously very fond of you,
a lady by the name of Sue, who lives in Oldham in Lancashire.
Good luck with your exams toward next year.
James Pacino of [redacted address], Gray's in Essex
is a Pink Floyd fan and wanted to hear his name on the air.
James, you just did.
And I played that Floyd track also for Brian and Jill
and it's Jill's birthday on Saturday.
Lots of luck, Jill. I hope you have a nice birthday.
The track you heard before that was from a new album out on Kirshner.
The album is by Kansas.
It's called Audio Visions and the track was Relentless.
This is a band called Straight Eight
and that was from our session by Girl School,
A track called Breakdown. Kelly was doing the lead vocals.
And prior to that, you heard something from a new album called Shuffle and Cut.
It's by Straight Eight. It's out on Logo.
And the track we played was On the Rebound.
Five minutes exactly to eleven.
Can I now tell you about something that's happening on this program in two weeks' time?
I mentioned it last week, and many thanks to everybody who responded.
At a fortnight's time, we're going to do Flashback 1970,
part of our occasional series of flashback programs
when we review, according to what you like,
some of the rock tracks of the year that we have in mind.
As I said, it's Flashback 1970 in a fortnight's time.
If there's any track that you'd like us to play, let us know.
You can put it, incidentally, if you like,
on your Friday Night Connection cards and letters.
That's coming up very shortly.
We don't mind you doing that at all.
And from our 1977 session by Coliseum,
I think this is the best-remembered track of that session.
It's called Intergalactic Strut.
John Heisman on the drums, Gary Morley guitar,
[Music]
Don Airy who wrote that piece on the keyboards
And John Moe on the bass
That was Coliseum 2, an intergalactic strut
It is time for the Friday Night Connection
Now last week the tracks that I played were these
Careful With That Axe, Eugene
By Pink Floyd from the LP Amagama
Throw Down the Sword by Wishbone Ash
From the LP Classic Ash
Six Blade Knife by Dire Straits from the LP
Find a theme, strand, or if you like just a Friday Night Connection
Night Connection for tonight, put together by Danny Berrigan of Rainhill in Merseyside.
Three bits of music, and in the three bits, or the titles thereof,
or maybe the titles of the album, or somebody who plays on the tracks.
It's definitely in there somewhere.
At the moment, it's for me to know, and to find out.
There is a connection, a Friday Night Connection.
If you think you know what it is, jot it down on a postcard, letter,
any form of communication you'd care to choose,
and send it to The Friday Night Connection.
The Friday Rock Show
BBC Radio 1
London W1A4WW
And the person whose card gets read out on the air next Friday
will get themselves a £10 record token.
Also on that card, letter, or any other form of communication you like
you can put your choice for Flashback 70
the programme we're going to be doing in a fortnight's time.
Your favourite track from the year of 1970.
Good luck.
It's 11.18 on the Friday Rock Show.
now here are a couple of tracks which you won't find on any record anywhere. Recorded
live in Jacksonville in Florida, this is the band called Blackfoot. Excellent bunch of
guys they are. Mainly made up of Native Americans is the band called Blackfoot, and a couple
of tracks you heard there recorded live in the hometown of Jacksonville in Florida, which
incidentally, those two versions are not available on record, though they do have two albums
out in this country, which I believe are on the Atco label. And at the moment, Blackfoot
are touring our country with the band called The Scorpions.
I think they're an ace band.
The tracks they played were On The Run and Gimme, Gimme, Gimme.
Let's go back to school with the girls.
[Music]
Status Quo had a track from their new album,
which is just out, the album called Just Supposing.
That track was entitled Don't Drive My Car.
And it was for all the Quo army around the country,
particularly all the good mates of a gentleman by the name of Tom Marchbanks,
who lives in Colgrane, Helensburgh in Scotland,
who wanted me to remember him to all the guys that he met from London
in Tragoed in Wales
and also a girl called Mandy.
And here's one that comes from
Tarpally, I think.
It isn't Cheshire.
It's for Tez the Lez
and Mike the Bike
and Peter the Streaker
and somebody else
and Don the Bone
and then it says PTO
from Lock with the Big...
I can't say that.
Before that, you heard something
by Girl School.
It was called Demolition
and this is Coliseum 2.
And that was Alvin Lee
[Music]
and his new band
from a new album
that's just come out on Avatar,
the album called Freefall.
The track you heard was One Lonely Hour.
And the album isn't the Alvin Lee that we've all grown to know and love
with super speed over the years, but it really is a great album.
And before that, you heard our final one tonight from our session by Coliseum 2,
the band that was together in 1967.
The track we played was Lament.
It was mighty tasty to hear again.
I love my calendar.
And the girls, we're not.
But there are 12 great colour pictures of the Radio 1 DJs at the circus on
The new 1981 Radio 1 calendar.
Yeah, we had plenty of fun when we went to the circus to be photographed for Radio 1's latest production.
And you can get your own copy by sending a cheque or a postal order for £1.95 to Radio 1 Office, P.O. Box 275, Portishead, Bristol, BS20, 9SG.
That's Radio 1 Office, P.O. Box 275, Portishead, Bristol, BS20, 9SG.
Everyone who buys one gets a special Radio 1 pencil and free car sticker.
And all of us will be personally autographing ten copies of the calendar each week.
And these will be sent to buyers whose names are drawn out of Mike Reid's top hat.
Don't forget to put your own name and address on your application for...
The new 1981 Radio 1 calendar.
And don't forget, each week one of us is going to be delivering a calendar personally to you, as you're aware,
especially if you're the person that Noel delivered it to last week in his helicopter.
We're getting more and more extravagant each week.
and this coming week
I've got everything I need
my cycle clips
my cycle vest
and my saddle bag
and I could well be
appearing in your town
and in your house
so get the food out eh
this is Mike Reed
and I could be at your place
next week with your calendar
oh that's nice
you want to see
Peeler and I
in that calendar
we're both made up
as clowns
which is quite appropriate
and that's Wishbone Ash
[Music]
from a brand new album
that's just come out
the album is called
Live Dates 2
the track you heard there
was called Runaway
rumour is rife
at the moment in the industry that there are going to be changes
in the Wishbone Ash line-up,
but I don't have any specific details for you at the moment.
Just as soon as I know them, I'll let you know.
But the rumour is very strong.
The first 25,000 copies of that LP have a free LP contained therein.
To close tonight, here's something by the remarkable Mr Neil Young.
[Music]
[Music]
First saw the light of day way back in 1975 on the album called Zuma.
That was Mr Neil Young and Cortez the Killer,
and it was for Andrew Cotton and all the kings of speed
who live in Holmes Chapel in Cheshire.
Right, on Rock on Saturday tomorrow, 4 to 7.30,
Nash the Slash, Joe Jackson, Mark Knopfler of Dire Straits
and the Yellow Magic Orchestra will be speaking.
Next week on the Friday Rock Show, no sessions at all.
It'll be all records, including some nice, tasty imports.
Don't forget Two's Up, our birthday program upcoming.
We'd like your favorite rock track of all time on a separate card, please,
to this program.
Our engineers tonight were Keith and Neil.
Thank you, gentlemen.
And our address is Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Following the time signal for midnight, the 285... 275... 285 transmitters are closing down.
But you know that anyway, and they'll be back on the air tomorrow morning.
This is Tony Wilson. Hello.
Tonight, a special goodnight to all teddy bears who fell asleep last week.
Oh, really? That must have been a very disappointing point.
God bless everybody. I'm Tommy Vance in London.
Wherever you are, I hope you have a very good night.
Take care. Good night.
